overlapAnalysis.summary: The 10 pilot topics span two domains (Medical Terminology and Anatomy & Physiology) with no accidental domain cross-contamination. Within each domain, adjacent topics deliberately share certain bridge concepts — this is intentional prerequisite reinforcement, not problematic duplication. No question stem or answer rationale was found to be identical across topics.

intentionalBridgeConcepts.concept: Cardiovascular root words (cardi/o, hem/o, arteri/o, thromb/o)
intentionalBridgeConcepts.assessment: INTENTIONAL — prefixes-suffixes-root-words introduces these roots at the word-part level; cardiovascular-respiratory-terminology applies them in clinical context. The depth and application level differ. No duplication concern.
intentionalBridgeConcepts.action: None. Downstream topic correctly extends upstream vocabulary.

intentionalBridgeConcepts.assessment: INTENTIONAL — suffixes are introduced once in prefixes-suffixes-root-words, then applied in system-specific topics. Applied questions are distinct from definitional questions. Spaced repetition of core suffixes is pedagogically appropriate.
intentionalBridgeConcepts.action: None. Applied questions should emphasize system-specific context rather than re-testing bare definitions.

intentionalBridgeConcepts.assessment: INTENTIONAL — introduced abstractly in batch-01, applied in clinical context in batch-02. Question content is different (batch-01: define brady-; batch-02: clinical implication of bradycardia/bradypnea). No duplication concern.

INTENTIONAL — MI is used in cell-structure-and-function to illustrate apoptosis vs necrosis/troponin release; MI is used in cell-transport-mechanisms to illustrate Na+/K+ pump failure from ATP depletion. These are distinct mechanistic explanations using the same disease anchor. The teaching purpose is different in each lesson.
intentionalBridgeConcepts.action: Add brief cross-reference note in lesson teaching notes to acknowledge the connection and avoid student confusion (e.g., 'This builds on the MI mechanism introduced in cell-structure-and-function.').

intentionalBridgeConcepts.assessment: INTENTIONAL — nephr/o introduced as a root word example in batch-01; gi-urinary-terminology applies it to renal conditions (nephritis, pyelonephritis, nephrolithiasis). Application level and clinical context differ. No duplication concern.

MINOR OVERLAP — cell-structure-and-function mentions GLUT4 and insulin resistance in the membrane receptor section (Q22). cell-transport-mechanisms covers GLUT4/insulin in greater mechanistic depth (facilitated diffusion, SGLT2 inhibitors). The cell-structure-and-function treatment is introductory; the transport treatment is the primary home for this concept.
intentionalBridgeConcepts.action: RECOMMENDED: In cell-structure-and-function Q22, add a forward-reference hint ('explored in depth in cell-transport-mechanisms') in the teaching note. Ensure question difficulty remains focused on membrane receptors rather than transport mechanisms in that topic.

intentionalBridgeConcepts.concept: Neuron structure and neurological root words (neur/o, encephal/o)
intentionalBridgeConcepts.assessment: MINOR OVERLAP — musculoskeletal-neuro-terminology introduces neurological root words and decoding; four-tissue-types covers nervous tissue biology (neurons, neuroglia) at the tissue level. These are distinct lenses (vocabulary vs biology), so the content is complementary, not duplicative.
intentionalBridgeConcepts.action: None. Flag in teaching notes that students may connect word-part knowledge from the earlier topic.

intentionalBridgeConcepts.assessment: CONTAINED — MS appears only in the four-tissue-types topic as an application of oligodendrocyte/myelin function. No duplication.

INTENTIONAL PREREQUISITE — cell-structure-and-function introduces the plasma membrane concept (phospholipid bilayer, proteins, selective permeability). cell-transport-mechanisms builds directly on this by covering how substances cross that membrane. This is a designed prerequisite relationship, not duplication.
intentionalBridgeConcepts.action: None. This is the correct sequence.

nearDuplicateQuestions.description: Both questions test suffix -penia (deficiency). Q07 uses thrombocytopenia; Q10 also uses thrombocytopenia.
nearDuplicateQuestions.action: RECOMMENDED: Change cardiovascular-respiratory-terminology-Q10 answer options to use a different example condition (e.g., neutropenia or leukopenia) to avoid redundant test-taking logic. The clinical context and correct answer remain the same.

findings.finding: No NCLEX-brand language, exam-coaching phrasing, or pathway-specific nursing exam language detected across any of the 10 pilot topics.
All questions use foundational framing consistent with pre-nursing level learners. Questions test concept understanding and clinical application, not recall of exam-specific mnemonics.

Three questions involve pharmacology (SGLT2 inhibitors, digoxin, calcium channel blockers). Tone is appropriate — these are used as clinical applications of transport mechanisms, not as pharmacology questions. However, some Q stems assume familiarity with drug names that pre-nursing students may not yet have.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding brief drug name context in the question stem (e.g., 'empagliflozin, a class of medication called SGLT2 inhibitors') to scaffold for pre-nursing learners unfamiliar with these drug names.

findings.finding: Marfan syndrome and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome are referenced as connective tissue disorder examples. These are accurate and at appropriate depth for foundational learners.

findings: cell-transport-mechanisms and four-tissue-types have a higher proportion of applied questions (59% and 64%). This is appropriate given their direct clinical relevance, but a few additional foundational-recall questions may be warranted to serve learners who need vocabulary scaffolding.
musculoskeletal-neuro-terminology has the lowest applied proportion (32%). Consider adding 2-3 more clinically situated questions in a future revision.
Overall pilot applied percentage: 43% — well within acceptable range for foundational pre-nursing content (target 35–50% applied).
All 10 topic slugs match exactly between the blueprint JSON, batch report, and pilot artifact filenames.
No slug collision or case inconsistency detected.
All pilot artifact files are located in the correct batch subdirectory matching their batchId field.
